chunk_summary: Low-cost Indian diet densification strategies focus on energy-dense, culturally appropriate, and affordable meal modifications to address feeding difficulties and faltering growth in children, emphasizing adherence and nutrient-dense snack substitutions.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of culturally appropriate diet modifications in addressing faltering growth.
Learn strategies for energy densification in vegetarian and mixed diets using affordable ingredients.
Identify snack substitution options to improve nutrient intake and reduce sugary snacks.
Incorporate follow-up methods to assess adherence and adjust interventions.
key_facts: Milk and packaged snacks often displace family foods in Indian diets, reducing nutrient intake.
Vegetarian energy densification strategies include adding oil, ghee, groundnut paste, or sesame to meals.
Mixed diets can incorporate egg, fish, chicken, or meat gravies into rice or roti for energy density.
Snack substitutions include banana with curd, peanut chikki, egg, or thick lassi without added sugar.
Dental caries prevention aligns with reducing sugary snacks, drinks, and prolonged bottle feeding.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: A catch-up prescription must match the family’s budget and food culture or adherence will fail. (WHO Guideline: complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age, 2023)
